title: The Tech Support Trap
briefing: Detective, a retired engineer just called the Agency in a panic. A terrifying pop-up took over his screen, telling him his computer is infected and to call Microsoft immediately. But something in his gut said this wasn't right — and he was correct. Examine the evidence, then take it further: interrogate the scammer yourself.

evidence.html:
http://microsof-support-alert.win32virus.com/warning
⊞ Microsoft Windows
Security Alert

⚠ YOUR COMPUTER HAS BEEN INFECTED ⚠

TROJAN VIRUS DETECTED — ALL PERSONAL DATA AT RISK
DO NOT CLOSE THIS WINDOW OR TURN OFF YOUR COMPUTER

Your Windows system has been blocked due to suspicious activity. Your personal files, banking information, and passwords are being accessed by hackers right now.

CALL MICROSOFT SUPPORT IMMEDIATELY

1-800-555-0172

Available 24/7 — Do not delay

WARNING: Closing this window will result in immediate data loss and account suspension

hotspots.label: ALL-CAPS alarming language designed to induce panic
hotspots.explanation: Real Microsoft security alerts use calm, professional language. Aggressive all-caps warnings are designed to override your rational thinking with fear. If it's screaming at you, it's trying to manipulate you.

hotspots.label: Phone number — not on any official Microsoft page
hotspots.explanation: Microsoft's real support number is on microsoft.com — never in a pop-up. Any phone number in an alarm pop-up goes directly to scammers. Real Microsoft support doesn't work this way.

hotspots.label: Pop-up threatens consequences for closing — 'Do not close this window'
hotspots.explanation: Legitimate software never threatens you for closing a window. This is a trap designed to keep you panicked and on the page. You can always safely close your browser — it cannot actually harm your computer.

hotspots.label: Website URL is not microsoft.com
hotspots.explanation: Look at the browser address bar: 'microsof-support-alert.win32virus.com' — not microsoft.com. Scammers buy lookalike domain names to appear official. The real Microsoft never shows alerts from third-party sites.

deduction_options.text: Fake tech support scam — the unofficial URL, panic language, threat about closing the window, and unverified phone number confirm this is a trap, not a real Microsoft alert
Legitimate Microsoft security warning — the computer really is infected and needs immediate attention
Browser glitch — an accidental pop-up from a buggy website, nothing to worry about

learning_summary: Microsoft never contacts you through pop-up warnings. Never call a phone number shown in a browser alert. Force-quit your browser (Cmd+Q on Mac, Alt+F4 on Windows) to close uncloseable pop-ups. Real tech support is contacted by you — not the other way around.
